metadata.title: The Hardest Sentence: Your Tool in One Line
metadata.playlist: Say What It Is — The Conductor Brief
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5 brief — The Conductor Brief: Part 1 (12 pts)

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam here. Episode 1 of 7. The Assignment 5 brief has six sections, but only one of them generates every other one: Part 1 — the single sentence that says what your tool is. Twelve points. The grade math is simple: no sentence, no grade. The real math is this — the same sentence becomes your A6 positioning, your LinkedIn headline, and your pitch intro. Write a vague one now and you rewrite it four times over the next two assignments. This episode is the method: a formula that works, three worked examples that show you why it works, the specific tells that mark a sentence as broken, and a one-question test you can run in thirty seconds. One sentence. No partial credit for 'almost clear.' Let's get it right the first time.

props.segment: The Hardest Sentence: Your Tool in One Line
props.command: Write the single sentence that says exactly what your tool is, who it's for, and what they walk away able to do.
props.runningText: Part 1 feeds A6 positioning, LinkedIn, and every pitch — get it right now…
output: The formula: [Tool] helps [specific user] [action] so they can [outcome]
The 4 weak-sentence tells — tech description, feature list, jargon, dev-written
The read-aloud test: one question that tells you if you're done

beats.narration_text: The formula for Part 1 is: [Tool] helps [specific user] [specific action] so they can [specific outcome]. Four slots. Every weak sentence breaks at least one of them. Let me show you with two examples. Weak version: 'Our platform uses AI to optimize marketing workflows for teams.' That sentence fills zero slots correctly. 'Teams' is not a user. 'Optimize' is not an action. 'Marketing workflows' is not an outcome — it is a category. Formula version: 'Our tool helps email marketers at B2C startups A/B-test subject lines so they can find the version that actually gets opened before the campaign goes out.' Now every slot is filled. Specific user — email marketers at B2C startups. Specific action — A/B-test subject lines. Specific outcome — find the version that gets opened before the campaign goes out. The outcome slot is where most sentences fail. Outcomes describe what the user can DO or DECIDE after — not what the tool processes. 'Processes marketing data' is not an outcome. 'Know which campaign to run on Monday morning' is.

artifactLines: Weak: 'Our platform uses AI to optimize marketing workflows for teams.' — zero slots filled.
Strong: 'Our tool helps email marketers at B2C startups A/B-test subject lines so they can find the version that gets opened before the campaign goes out.'
Specific user: 'email marketers at B2C startups' — a real role in a real company type.
Specific action: 'A/B-test subject lines' — one capability, not a category.
Specific outcome: what the user can DO or DECIDE after — not what the tool processes.

beats.narration_text: Three examples — one for each common A2 problem type. Research assistant: weak version — 'An AI tool that helps researchers find relevant papers faster.' Formula version — 'Our tool helps PhD students in clinical research surface the three most-cited papers for any hypothesis they are testing, so they can skip the 40-paper manual review and start writing in an hour instead of a day.' News briefing tool: weak — 'An automated news summarizer for professionals.' Formula version — 'Our tool helps investment analysts who cover three to five sectors get a five-bullet brief on each sector every morning, so they can walk into the nine AM meeting knowing what moved last night.' Social monitoring tool: weak — 'An AI-powered social listening platform.' Formula version — 'Our tool helps brand managers at mid-size consumer goods companies catch negative threads about their product before the thread hits five hundred reposts, so they can respond in the conversation window instead of playing defense.' In each case: specific user, specific action, specific outcome. The outcome is always something the user does or decides — not something the tool does.

artifactLines: Research: 'Our tool helps PhD students in clinical research surface the 3 most-cited papers for any hypothesis, so they can skip the 40-paper review and start writing in an hour.'
Briefing: 'Our tool helps investment analysts covering 3–5 sectors get a 5-bullet brief on each sector every morning, so they walk into the 9AM meeting knowing what moved.'
Monitoring: 'Our tool helps brand managers catch negative threads before they hit 500 reposts, so they respond in the conversation window instead of playing defense.'
Pattern: user is a role in a context — action is one capability — outcome is what they can do after.
props.sparkLine: The outcome is what the user decides — not what the tool does.

beats.narration_text: There are four tells that mark a sentence as broken — the graders know all four. Tell one: describes technology, not outcome. 'Leverages large language models to generate personalized content' is a technology description. The grader cannot tell who this is for or what they can do after. Tell two: feature list. 'Summarizes, categorizes, and prioritizes content with AI' is three things that do not add up to one outcome. If you list features, rewrite until there is one outcome. Tell three: jargon. 'Optimizes,' 'leverages,' 'utilizes,' 'AI-powered,' 'scalable' — these words tell the grader you have not decided what the tool actually does for a real person. Each one is a rewrite flag. Tell four: written for a developer, not a user. 'Processes text inputs via API to return structured JSON' — zero humans mentioned, zero outcomes stated. The fix for every tell is the same: return to the formula. Who is the specific user? What specific action does the tool enable? What can they do or decide after?

artifactLines: Tell 1 — Tech description, not outcome: 'Leverages LLMs to generate personalized content.' Who? What do they walk away with?
Tell 2 — Feature list: 'Summarizes, categorizes, and prioritizes content.' Three features ≠ one outcome.
Tell 3 — Jargon: 'Optimizes / leverages / AI-powered / scalable.' These words mean you haven't decided what the tool does for a real person.
Tell 4 — Written for a developer: 'Processes text inputs via API to return structured JSON.' No human, no outcome.
Fix: return to the formula every time — specific user, specific action, specific outcome.
props.sparkLine: Any one of the four tells is a rewrite.

beats.narration_text: The read-aloud test is the whole method for Part 1 — and it takes thirty seconds. Say your sentence to someone who has never heard of your project. Not a friend who has sat through three project updates. A roommate, a family member, someone with no context. One of two things happens. Either they say 'oh, that's useful' — in which case you are close. Or they say 'what does that mean?' — in which case you rewrite. That is the entire test. It catches every tell at once: jargon fails the test because it confuses people. Feature lists fail because they do not land as useful. Tech descriptions fail because the listener hears a tool, not a benefit. The read-aloud test also forces you to say the sentence out loud, which is different from reading it on a screen. Sentences that look fine in writing often break when spoken. Read it aloud before you run the test on someone else. If you stumble on your own sentence, the listener will too.

artifactLines: Say your sentence to someone who has never heard of your project.
Not a team member — a roommate, a family member, zero context.
'Oh, that's useful' → you're close. 'What does that mean?' → rewrite.
Jargon fails this test. Feature lists fail this test. Tech descriptions fail this test.
Say the sentence out loud before you test it on someone else — if you stumble, they will too.
Part 1 has no partial credit for 'almost clear.' This test tells you when you're there.
props.sparkLine: 'Oh, that's useful' = close. 'What does that mean?' = rewrite.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Part 1. The sentence is: [Tool] helps [specific user] [specific action] so they can [specific outcome]. Fill all four slots. The outcome describes what the user can do or decide after — not what the tool processes. The four weak-sentence tells are: technology description not outcome, feature list, jargon, written for a developer. Any one earns a rewrite request before A6. The test is the read-aloud test: say it to someone with zero context. 'Oh, that's useful' means you are close. 'What does that mean?' means rewrite. Your deliverable is one sentence. The time to get it right is now, before it becomes the foundation of the next five sections and your A6 positioning.

props.artifactTitle: The Hardest Sentence: Your Tool in One Line
artifactLines: Formula: [Tool] helps [specific user] [specific action] so they can [specific outcome].
The outcome = what the user can DO or DECIDE after — not what the tool processes.
4 weak-sentence tells: tech description · feature list · jargon · written for a developer.
The read-aloud test: say it to zero-context listener → 'that's useful' = close; 'what?' = rewrite.
This sentence feeds Part 1 coherence check, A6 positioning, LinkedIn, and your pitch.
No partial credit for 'almost clear.' One sentence. Get it right before E2.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude — fill in the brackets with your actual A2 problem before you run it. The prompt reads: I am building this tool: [paste your A2 problem statement, one to two sentences]. Generate five candidate one-sentences using the formula: '[Tool] helps [specific user] [specific action] so they can [specific outcome].' For each: name the specific user role, the specific action the tool enables, and the specific outcome — meaning, what the user can do or decide after, not what the tool processes. Then score each on a scale of one to five on specificity: a stranger who has never seen my project description should understand exactly who this is for and what they walk away able to do. After scoring, write one combined version that takes the strongest elements from the highest-scoring candidates. That prompt gives you a first draft. Then run the read-aloud test: say it to someone who has not seen your project. 'That's useful' means close. 'What does that mean?' means one more rewrite. Do not submit a sentence you have not tested aloud.

props.command: I am building this tool: [paste your A2 problem statement]. Generate 5 candidate one-sentences using the formula: '[Tool] helps [specific user] [action] so they can [outcome].' Score each 1–5 on specificity (a stranger should understand who it's for and what they walk away able to do). Then write one combined version from the highest-scoring candidates.
props.runningText: fill in your A2 problem before running — the formula only works with a real user and a real outcome…

beats.narration_text: The Hardest Sentence: Your Tool in One Line. Liam, in for Bear.
